name: Equatorial Guinea
tagline: From the Bubi of Bioko and the Fang of Río Muni, through the Portuguese sighting of Fernando Pó, Spain's cocoa colony and a British anti-slavery base, to independence in 1968, the terror of Macías Nguema, the long rule of his nephew Obiang, and the oil wealth of the 1990s.
summary: Equatorial Guinea is the only sovereign state in mainland Africa with Spanish as an official language, made up of the volcanic island of Bioko (long called Fernando Pó), the small island of Annobón, several islets, and the mainland enclave of Río Muni between Cameroon and Gabon. Bioko is the homeland of the Bantu-speaking Bubi; Río Muni is dominated by the Fang, with Ndowe and related "Playero" peoples on the coast, while the Annobonese descend from people brought by the Portuguese from Angola and São Tomé. Portuguese navigators sighted Bioko in 1472, naming it for the explorer Fernão do Pó, and held the islands until ceding them, with commercial rights on the adjacent coast, to Spain by the Treaty of El Pardo in 1778. Britain ran an anti-slave-trade base on the island from 1827 to 1843; Spain established effective administration only from 1858 and built a cocoa-plantation economy on Fernando Pó worked by contract labourers from Liberia and Nigeria. United from 1926 as the colony of Spanish Guinea, the territory became independent on 12 October 1968. Its first president, Francisco Macías Nguema, imposed an eleven-year reign of terror in which tens of thousands were killed and a large share of the population fled into exile, until his nephew Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo overthrew and executed him in 1979. Obiang has ruled ever since, his tenure transformed by the discovery of major offshore oil from the mid-1990s, which produced soaring wealth alongside deep inequality.

founding.label: Independence from Spain
founding.detail: The Republic of Equatorial Guinea became independent from Spain on 12 October 1968, ending nearly two centuries of Spanish claims that began with the 1778 Treaty of El Pardo. Spain, under United Nations and nationalist pressure, had moved the territory from colony to overseas "province" in 1959 and granted internal autonomy after a 1963 referendum; a constitutional referendum in 1968 preceded full sovereignty. Francisco Macías Nguema, who won the September 1968 presidential election, became the new state's first president. The country it inherited combined two very different lands: the island of Bioko (Fernando Pó), with its Bubi people and Spanish-built cocoa plantations, and the mainland enclave of Río Muni, home to the Fang and coastal Ndowe peoples — together with the small islands of Annobón and the islets of the Gulf of Guinea.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: From Spain on 12 October 1968; first president Francisco Macías Nguema

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Malabo, on Bioko Island; called Santa Isabel until 1973, and earlier Port Clarence

quickFacts.label: Geography
quickFacts.value: Island of Bioko (formerly Fernando Pó) and Annobón plus the mainland enclave of Río Muni, between Cameroon and Gabon

quickFacts.label: Peoples
quickFacts.value: Bubi on Bioko; Fang and coastal Ndowe ("Playeros") in Río Muni; Annobonese on Annobón

quickFacts.label: Language
quickFacts.value: The only African state with Spanish as an official language; French and Portuguese are also official

quickFacts.label: Economy
quickFacts.value: A Spanish cocoa colony; since the mid-1990s a major offshore oil and gas producer, with high GDP per capita but wide inequality

eras.title: Peoples of the island and the mainland
eras.standfirst: Bantu-speaking peoples settled the island of Bioko and the forests of Río Muni over many centuries: the Bubi on the island, the Fang and Ndowe on the mainland coast, with the Annobonese a later, Portuguese-introduced community.
body: The lands that became Equatorial Guinea were peopled over a long span by Bantu-speaking groups moving out of the Cameroon–Río Muni region and across the Gulf of Guinea. Bioko — the volcanic island long known to Europeans as Fernando Pó — became the home of the Bubi, who are generally described as descendants of Bantu-speakers who crossed to the island from the nearby mainland in successive waves, by some accounts from around the thirteenth century, settling an island that earlier held Neolithic populations. Cut off by the sea, the Bubi developed a distinct society, language and political organisation of their own.

On the mainland enclave of Río Muni, between present-day Cameroon and Gabon, the dominant people are the Fang, a large Bantu group whose southward and westward migrations brought them into the equatorial forests; isolated pockets of earlier forest hunter-gatherers survived in the north. Along the Río Muni coast lived the Ndowe and related communities — Kombe, Benga, Bujeba and others — sometimes grouped together by the Spanish term Playeros, "beach people," who became intermediaries between the interior and the sea.

The small, remote island of Annobón, far to the southwest, was uninhabited until the era of Atlantic navigation; its population, the Annobonese, descends from Africans brought there by the Portuguese, largely from Angola by way of São Tomé, and speaks a Portuguese-based creole. From these distinct strands — Bubi islanders, Fang and Ndowe of the mainland, and the Annobonese — would be assembled, centuries later, a single colonial and then national territory.

events.title: Bubi settle Bioko
events.summary: Bantu-speaking peoples, ancestors of the Bubi, are held to have crossed from the mainland to the island later called Fernando Pó (Bioko) in successive waves, settling an island that earlier held Neolithic populations and developing a distinct island society.

events.title: Fang and Ndowe of Río Muni
events.summary: The Fang, a large Bantu people, settled the forests of the mainland enclave of Río Muni, while Ndowe and related coastal peoples — later grouped as Playeros — occupied the shoreline and mediated trade with the interior.

events.title: Annobón peopled from Angola
events.summary: The remote island of Annobón, uninhabited before Atlantic navigation, was settled by Africans brought by the Portuguese largely from Angola via São Tomé; the Annobonese came to speak a Portuguese-based creole.

pullquote.text: Two lands and several peoples — the Bubi of the island, the Fang and Ndowe of the mainland, the Annobonese of a far southern isle — long before they shared a single name.

eras.title: Fernão do Pó, the Portuguese, and the cession to Spain
eras.standfirst: The Portuguese navigator Fernão do Pó sighted Bioko in 1472 and named it Formosa; Portugal held the islands until ceding Fernando Pó, Annobón and rights on the adjacent coast to Spain by the Treaty of El Pardo in 1778.
body: In 1472 the Portuguese explorer Fernão do Pó, sailing the Gulf of Guinea in search of a route to India, became the first European on record to sight the great volcanic island that would bear his name. He is said to have called it Formosa — "beautiful" — but it soon took the name Fernando Pó (later Hispanicised, and ultimately renamed Bioko). Portugal claimed Fernando Pó and the smaller island of Annobón and drew them into the Atlantic world that linked São Tomé, Angola and Brazil, though it built little lasting settlement on Fernando Pó itself, where the Bubi remained largely self-governing.

The decisive change of sovereignty came in 1778. By the Treaty of El Pardo, signed on 11 March 1778 between Queen Maria I of Portugal and King Charles III of Spain, Portugal ceded to Spain the islands of Fernando Pó and Annobón, together with commercial rights on the African mainland coast of the Bight of Biafra. The exchange was part of a wider settlement of imperial boundaries in which Spain recognised Portuguese claims in South America; for Spain, the islands promised a base for the slave trade to its American colonies.

Spanish possession proved nominal at first. An expedition under the Conde de Argelejo reached the region in 1778 but was wrecked by disease and disorder, and Spain effectively withdrew. For decades the territory was administered, on paper, from distant Spanish America — at first under the Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata at Buenos Aires — while on the ground the islands lay open to other European traders. Real Spanish control of Fernando Pó would not come for another eighty years.

events.title: Fernão do Pó sights the island
events.summary: The Portuguese navigator Fernão do Pó sighted the volcanic island in the Gulf of Guinea in 1472, reportedly naming it Formosa; it soon took his own name as Fernando Pó, the island later renamed Bioko.

events.title: Treaty of El Pardo cedes the islands to Spain
events.summary: By the Treaty of El Pardo, Portugal ceded Fernando Pó, Annobón and commercial rights on the adjacent African coast to Spain, in exchange for recognition of Portuguese claims in South America; Spain sought a base for the slave trade.

events.title: The Argelejo expedition fails
events.summary: A Spanish expedition under the Conde de Argelejo arrived to take possession but was broken by disease and disorder; Spain effectively abandoned the islands for decades, administering them on paper from its American viceroyalties.

pullquote.text: By the Treaty of El Pardo of 1778, Portugal ceded Fernando Pó and Annobón to Spain — yet for eighty years Spanish rule existed mainly on paper.

eras.title: The British base and the Spanish cocoa colony
eras.standfirst: Britain ran an anti-slave-trade base on Fernando Pó from 1827 to 1843, founding Port Clarence; Spain established effective rule from 1858 and built a cocoa-plantation economy worked by contract labour from Liberia and beyond.
body: Although Spain held the title, it was Britain that first put down roots on Fernando Pó. In 1827, with Spanish acquiescence, the Royal Navy established a base on the island to support its squadron suppressing the Atlantic slave trade. William Fitzwilliam Owen founded a settlement at Port Clarence (later Santa Isabel, today Malabo), the natural harbour from which British cruisers intercepted slave ships and landed liberated Africans, many of whom — together with Sierra Leoneans and other West Africans — settled on the island as the English-speaking "Fernandino" Creole community. The British presence lasted until 1843, when the base was withdrawn to Sierra Leone, Spain declining to sell its rights.

Spain at last asserted itself in 1858, sending the naval officer Carlos Chacón as governor to organise a proper colonial administration; Baptist missionaries established under the British were soon pressed to withdraw in favour of Spanish Catholic missions. Over the following decades Spain extended claims onto the mainland of Río Muni, though the 1900 Treaty of Paris with France fixed its mainland enclave at a fraction of what it had sought — roughly 26,000 square kilometres rather than the far larger area once claimed.

The colony's wealth came to rest on cocoa. The rich volcanic soils of Fernando Pó were planted with cacao, and by the early twentieth century the island had become a significant cocoa producer dependent on imported labour, the Bubi population being too small and unwilling to supply the plantations. Spain recruited contract workers from abroad — a 1914 labour treaty with Liberia brought thousands of Liberians, and over time large numbers of Nigerians followed — under conditions that an International Labour Organization inquiry in 1930 found amounted, in the Liberian case, to recruitment by "criminal compulsion" scarcely distinguishable from slave raiding.

events.title: Britain founds an anti-slavery base at Port Clarence
events.summary: With Spanish acquiescence, the Royal Navy under William Fitzwilliam Owen established a base on Fernando Pó in 1827, founding Port Clarence (later Santa Isabel/Malabo) to support the squadron suppressing the Atlantic slave trade.

events.title: Britain withdraws to Sierra Leone
events.summary: The British base on Fernando Pó was withdrawn to Sierra Leone in 1843, Spain having declined to sell its rights; freed Africans, Sierra Leoneans and other settlers remained as the English-speaking Fernandino community.

events.title: Spain installs effective rule under Carlos Chacón
events.summary: Spain sent the naval officer Carlos Chacón as governor in 1858 to organise a colonial administration on Fernando Pó; Baptist missionaries established under the British were pressed to withdraw in favour of Spanish Catholic missions.

events.title: Treaty of Paris fixes Río Muni
events.summary: The 1900 Treaty of Paris with France delimited Spain's mainland enclave of Río Muni at roughly 26,000 km², far smaller than Spain had claimed, setting the borders with French Cameroon and Gabon.

events.title: Liberian labour treaty for the cocoa plantations
events.summary: A 1914 treaty brought contract labourers from Liberia to Fernando Pó's cocoa plantations; an ILO inquiry in 1930 found the recruitment had occurred under "criminal compulsion" akin to slave raiding, and the Liberian supply was cut off.

figures.name: William Fitzwilliam Owen
figures.role: Royal Navy officer, founder of Port Clarence
figures.blurb: British naval surveyor who in 1827 established the anti-slave-trade base on Fernando Pó and founded Port Clarence (later Santa Isabel, now Malabo), from which cruisers intercepted slave ships and landed liberated Africans.

pullquote.text: Liberated from slave ships at Port Clarence, then bound to the cocoa estates by contract — Fernando Pó's plantation economy ran on imported labour.
pullquote.attribution: drawn from ILO findings on Spanish Guinea, 1930

eras.title: Spanish Guinea and the road to independence
eras.standfirst: United from 1926 as the colony of Spanish Guinea, the island and mainland were governed as a cocoa-and-timber dependency until pressure for decolonisation carried the territory to independence on 12 October 1968.
body: From 1926 Spain administered Fernando Pó and Río Muni together as a single colony, Spanish Guinea, ruling through a small administration backed by Catholic missions and military campaigns that "pacified" the Fang of the mainland by the end of the 1920s. The cocoa estates of Fernando Pó remained the economic heart of the colony, and as Liberian recruitment ended, Nigerian labour took its place: by the late 1960s something approaching a hundred thousand Nigerian workers — many from the Eastern Region — were present in the territory, making the plantation workforce overwhelmingly foreign.

After the Second World War, as European empires came under growing pressure to decolonise, Spain reorganised the territory's status. In 1959 it declared Guinea assimilated and reclassified the colony as the "Equatorial Region" of Spain, divided into the provinces of Fernando Poo and Río Muni, with elected representatives sent to the Spanish Cortes. A further step came in 1963, when a referendum approved a "regime of autonomy" granting limited internal self-government, and the territory took the name Equatorial Guinea.

Under United Nations supervision and rising nationalist demands, Spain moved to full decolonisation in 1968. A constitutional referendum that summer was followed by elections in September, won by the nationalist politician Francisco Macías Nguema. On 12 October 1968 the Republic of Equatorial Guinea became independent, uniting the Bubi island of Bioko, the Fang and Ndowe mainland of Río Muni, and the island of Annobón into a single sovereign state — and making Spanish its language of government.

events.title: Bioko and Río Muni united as Spanish Guinea
events.summary: From 1926 Spain governed Fernando Pó and the mainland enclave of Río Muni together as the colony of Spanish Guinea, its economy resting on the cocoa plantations of the island.

events.title: Made an overseas "province" of Spain
events.summary: In 1959 Spain reclassified the colony as the "Equatorial Region," divided into the provinces of Fernando Poo and Río Muni, with elected local representatives sent to the Spanish Cortes — a first step toward decolonisation.

events.title: Autonomy and the name Equatorial Guinea
events.summary: A 1963 referendum approved a regime of autonomy granting limited internal self-government; the territory took the name Equatorial Guinea ahead of full independence.

events.title: Independence from Spain
events.summary: After a 1968 constitutional referendum and September elections won by Francisco Macías Nguema, the Republic of Equatorial Guinea became independent from Spain on 12 October 1968, uniting Bioko, Río Muni and Annobón.

pullquote.text: On 12 October 1968 the Bubi island, the Fang mainland and Annobón became one republic — and the only African state to take Spanish as its official tongue.

eras.title: The terror of Macías Nguema
eras.standfirst: Independence gave way within months to the eleven-year dictatorship of Francisco Macías Nguema — a reign of killings, forced labour and flight in which a large share of the population died or fled, until his nephew overthrew and executed him in 1979.
body: The republic's first president, Francisco Macías Nguema, swiftly turned the new state into a personal tyranny. He suppressed opposition, declared a single-party state in 1970, abrogated constitutional protections, and in 1972 had himself proclaimed president for life. He cultivated a violent personality cult, dismantling churches, schools and much of the administration, and directing terror against political rivals, the Bubi minority, Nigerian workers and the educated class. Observers came to describe the country, with grim hyperbole, as the "Dachau of Africa."

The human toll was catastrophic for so small a country. Estimates commonly hold that tens of thousands of people — figures of around 35,000 to 50,000 deaths are frequently cited, against a population of only a few hundred thousand — were killed during Macías's rule, while a comparable or larger number fled abroad; by 1979 roughly a quarter of the population is reckoned to have been living in exile. The cocoa economy collapsed as the foreign workforce departed: in the mid-1970s tens of thousands of Nigerian labourers were withdrawn amid the violence, and plantation production fell away. These remain estimates from a period of secrecy and disorder, and figures vary between sources.

The regime ended in August 1979, when Macías's own nephew, Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo — a senior army officer who had run the notorious Black Beach prison and whose own family had suffered under the dictator — led a coup against him. Macías was captured, tried, and executed by firing squad on 29 September 1979, alongside several associates. Obiang took power, beginning a rule that would prove the longest in the country's history.

events.title: Macías Nguema becomes first president
events.summary: Francisco Macías Nguema, victor of the September 1968 elections, became Equatorial Guinea's first president at independence, soon concentrating power and beginning a slide into dictatorship.

events.title: President for life
events.summary: After establishing a single-party state in 1970 and gutting the constitution, Macías had himself declared president for life in 1972, ruling through a violent personality cult that dismantled churches, schools and much of the state.

events.title: Mass killing and exile
events.summary: Under Macías tens of thousands were killed — figures of roughly 35,000–50,000 deaths are commonly cited — and by 1979 around a quarter of the population had fled into exile; the Bubi and Nigerian workers were among those targeted. Figures remain estimates.

events.title: Cocoa economy collapses as labourers flee
events.summary: Amid the terror, tens of thousands of Nigerian contract labourers were withdrawn from Fernando Pó's plantations in the mid-1970s, and cocoa production — the colony's economic mainstay — collapsed.

events.title: Obiang's coup overthrows Macías
events.summary: On 3 August 1979 Macías's nephew, army officer Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo, led a coup that overthrew the dictator after fighting that cost hundreds of lives.

events.title: Macías executed
events.summary: Captured and tried, Francisco Macías Nguema was executed by firing squad on 29 September 1979 along with several associates, ending eleven years of dictatorship; Obiang assumed the presidency.

figures.name: Francisco Macías Nguema
figures.blurb: Equatorial Guinea's first president, from 1968, who built a personal tyranny — a single-party state, a president-for-life cult, and mass killings and exile that devastated a country of only a few hundred thousand people, until he was overthrown and executed in 1979.

figures.name: Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo
figures.blurb: Senior army officer and nephew of Macías who had run the Black Beach prison; he led the August 1979 coup against his uncle, had him executed, and took the presidency — beginning a rule that became one of the world's longest.

pullquote.text: So total was the destruction that observers called Equatorial Guinea the "Dachau of Africa"; by 1979 a quarter of its people had fled into exile.

eras.title: Obiang, oil, and inequality
eras.standfirst: In power since 1979 and among the world's longest-serving leaders, Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo has presided over a country transformed by major offshore oil discovered from the mid-1990s — bringing sudden wealth and stark inequality.
body: Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo, who seized power in 1979, has ruled Equatorial Guinea ever since, making him one of the world's longest-serving heads of state. His government formally reintroduced multiparty politics in the early 1990s, but elections have been overwhelmingly won by Obiang and the ruling party amid opposition complaints and international criticism, and power has remained tightly held by the president and his circle. The renamed islands of Bioko and Annobón, the capital Malabo, and the mainland Río Muni were knit into a single, highly centralised state.

The country's trajectory was changed utterly by oil. Mobil and its partners discovered the large Zafiro field offshore Bioko in March 1995, with production beginning in August 1996; further discoveries followed. Within a few years Equatorial Guinea had become one of sub-Saharan Africa's leading oil producers, output rising from a few thousand barrels a day to a peak in the hundreds of thousands, and the small nation came to record one of the highest figures for gross domestic product per capita in Africa.

Yet the oil wealth has been distributed with extreme inequality. Despite headline GDP figures, a large part of the population has continued to live in poverty, and the country has drawn sustained criticism over corruption, human-rights conditions and the concentration of resources within the ruling family — including international legal cases targeting assets linked to the president's son and chosen successor. Equatorial Guinea thus remains a state of sharp contrasts: rich in petroleum, governed for over four decades by one man, and still shadowed by the legacy of its first, terrible decade of independence.

events.title: Obiang takes power
events.summary: Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo assumed the presidency after the 1979 coup and execution of Macías, and renamed the main islands Bioko and Annobón; he has remained in power ever since.

events.title: Zafiro oil field discovered
events.summary: Mobil and partners discovered the major Zafiro oil field offshore Bioko Island in March 1995, the find that opened Equatorial Guinea's petroleum era.

events.title: Oil production begins
events.summary: The Zafiro field came on-stream in August 1996, less than eighteen months after discovery, launching Equatorial Guinea as an oil producer; output later peaked in the hundreds of thousands of barrels a day.

events.title: The "Wonga Coup" foiled
events.summary: An attempted coup by a group of foreign mercenaries — the so-called Wonga Coup, linked to Simon Mann — was foiled in 2004, underscoring the instability around oil-rich Equatorial Guinea.

events.title: Wealth amid deep inequality
events.summary: Oil gave Equatorial Guinea one of Africa's highest GDP-per-capita figures, yet much of the population remained poor; the government faced sustained criticism over corruption, human rights and the concentration of wealth in the ruling family.

figures.name: Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo
figures.blurb: Seized power from his uncle Macías in 1979 and has ruled ever since, among the world's longest-serving leaders; his tenure was transformed by offshore oil from the mid-1990s, bringing high GDP per capita alongside enduring poverty, corruption charges and human-rights criticism.

pullquote.text: Oil made Equatorial Guinea, by one measure, among the richest countries per head in Africa — while much of its population stayed poor.

figures.name: Fernão do Pó
figures.blurb: Portuguese explorer who sighted the great volcanic island of the Gulf of Guinea in 1472, reportedly naming it Formosa; the island took his name as Fernando Pó before being renamed Bioko.

figures.name: Francisco Macías Nguema
figures.blurb: Equatorial Guinea's first president, whose eleven-year tyranny killed tens of thousands and drove a large share of the population into exile, until his nephew overthrew and executed him in 1979.

figures.name: Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo
figures.blurb: Overthrew his uncle Macías in 1979 and has ruled ever since, one of the world's longest-serving leaders; presided over the oil boom from the mid-1990s and the wealth and inequality it brought.

sources.label: Zafiro Oil Field, Bioko Island, Gulf of Guinea
Equatorial Guinea becomes an oil producer
